title: Multi-Head Latent Attention
description: An attention variant that compresses key-value cache storage into a low-rank latent space while keeping distinct query heads.
openingSummary: Multi-head latent attention (MLA) is an attention variant that stores a compact latent key-value representation and reconstructs per-head keys and values when needed, reducing cache size without collapsing to one shared head.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Multi-head latent attention (MLA) is an attention variant derived from multi-head attention. It keeps multiple query heads but projects keys and values into a shared low-rank latent space that is cached during autoregressive decoding.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: MLA targets key-value cache size, memory bandwidth during autoregressive decoding, and the cost of serving long contexts when full per-head key-value tensors would otherwise dominate memory.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: Keys and values are down-projected into a latent cache with rank r. During attention, those latent vectors are up-projected back to per-head key and value spaces. Queries remain head-specific, so each head attends against reconstructed key-value pairs derived from the shared latent cache.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: With multiple query heads and latent rank r, MLA caches compact latent vectors and reconstructs per-head keys and values through low-rank projections. The formulas below contrast full multi-head attention against MLA, which routes queries through a shared latent cache.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with multi-head attention, MLA stores a compressed latent cache instead of full per-head key-value tensors. Compared with multi-query and grouped-query attention, MLA keeps distinct query heads while compressing key-value state through low-rank projection rather than only sharing heads.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: MLA appears in large decoder-only language models that prioritize efficient long-context inference while retaining multi-head query pathways.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Low-rank key-value compression can limit representational flexibility versus storing full per-head tensors. The optimal latent rank depends on model scale and quality targets.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: As context windows grow, key-value cache efficiency remains a first-order serving constraint, so MLA offers a path between multi-head fidelity and aggressive cache compression through latent storage.

mla.queryHeadFlexibility: H distinct query heads with latent key-value reconstruction

mha.kvRepresentation: H key heads and H value heads
mha.queryHeadFlexibility: H independent query and key-value head pairs

mqa.queryHeadFlexibility: H query heads share one key-value pair

gqa.kvRepresentation: G key heads and G value heads
gqa.queryHeadFlexibility: H distinct query heads grouped into G shared key-value pairs

k.definition: Reconstructed key vectors from latent cache c.

v.definition: Reconstructed value vectors from latent cache c.

g.definition: Latent rank of the compressed key-value cache.

dk.definition: Key dimension per head after up-projection.

gi.definition: Shared latent key-value cache vector stored per token.

mhaKvHeads.label: H key-value head pairs (1:1 with queries)

mhaOneToOne.label: Each query head has its own K and V

mlaLowRankProjection.label: Up-project latent cache to per-head K and V

mlaReconstructionNote.label: A compact latent cache is expanded back into per-head keys and values
